english: the hind part, that which comes after, the later or latter part; slád-mar (slad mar), slád-kyis (slad kyis) afterwards, hereafter, slád-mar yaṅ (slad mar yang) also for the future. — 3. slád-du (slad du) on account of, for the sake of.

pf. bslad (bslad), (cf. lhád-pa (lhad pa)) to mix, esp. with something of an inferior quality, hence to adulterate, vitiate, to spoil, to corrupt, skyón-gyis (skyon gyis) or lhád-kyis ma slád-pa (lhad kyis ma slad pa) not marred by any defects Lex., pyin-ci-lóg-gis yoṅs-su slád-de (phyin ci log gis yongs su slad de) quite unfitted by perversity Dzl.; gáṅ-gis kyaṅ ma slád-pa (gang gis kyang ma slad pa) without any thing detrimental operating, not subject to any noxious influence Wdn.; de myós-śiṅ slád-par byás-te (de myos shing slad par byas te) making him drunk and thus disabling him Dzl.
1. (?) *na-slán* (*na slan*) W. the furred ear-coverings of Tibetan ladies. — 2. = slad (slad); slan-čád (slan chad) = pyin-čád (phyin chad).
1. to mend, patch Sch. — 2. v. bslan (bslan).
1. to roast slightly, to parch, to make brown by exposing to heat, e.g. meal C., W. — 2. to roast, to fry, *már-la* (*mar la*) W.

eleg. = pyir (phyir), 1. again, over again, once more. — 2. afterwards, hereafter, slár-nas (slar nas) id. C. — slar yaṅ ‛jug-pa (slar yang 'jug pa) to be affixed or added again (of letters, to the end of a word) Gram.; slar ‛óṅ-ba (slar 'ong ba) to come back, to return Dzl.; slar - gśegs (slar gshegs) he went away again Dzl.; slar stobs skyed (slar stobs skyed) he regains strength S.g.; slár-bsdu-ba (slar bsdu ba) the final o of a verb, indicating the end of a sentence Gram.
1. v. lhas (lhas). -- 2. retinue, train, attendants, wives and servants, p'o-bráṅ-gi slas (pho brang gi slas) a king's or prince's retinue, the court, people at court Dzl.

C. acc. to some authorities: a yellowish red apple, or Indian apple (opp. to kù-śu (ku shu) Tibetan apple); acc. to Cs. cherry; cherries, however, are scarcely known in Tibet. — sli-tsí (sli tsi) small, wild-growing, cherry-like dwarf-apples, Pyrus baccata; *bi-li-tsi* (*bi li tsi*) W. gooseberry; *wám-pu-li-tsi* (*wam pu li tsi*) the white berries of a species of mountain-ash, Pyrus ursina (*wampu* in the Bunan language: 'bear').

pf. bslus (bslus), fut. bslu (bslu), imp. (b)slu(s) ((b)slu(s)), to entice, allure, ensnare, beguile, seduce, e.g. to be ensnared by wordly sorrows; less frq. in a direct sense: to impose on, to deceive, rdzun byás-te (rdzun byas te) by a falsehood Dzl.; slu-k'rid (slu khrid) enticement, seduction, means of seduction, bait; bzáṅ-poí slu-k'rid (bzang po'i slu khrid) enticement to a good purpose; bslú-ba-mk'an (bslu ba mkhan) deceiver, deluder, impostor Glr.; mi-slú(-ba) (mi slu(-ba)) infallible, sure Mil.
1. a coarse blanket Ts., = čá-ra (cha ra), čá-ri (cha ri). — 2. n. of the capital of Ladak.
Med. n. of a creeper or climbing plant.
U, slé-ba (sle ba), slé-bo (sle bo) Cs. a flat basket.

I. vb., bsle-ba (bsle ba), lhé-ba (lhe ba). pf. lhas (lhas) B., *lá-če* (*la che*) W. to twist, plait, braid, the hair, (to make) a basket etc.; to knit. II. sbst. 1. v. no. I. — 2. distortion, dislocation (of a limb) Cs.; slé-bo (sle bo) one that has a distorted limb Cs.; sle-míg (sle mig) a distorted eye Cs.
craft, deceit, trickery, sle-yón byéd-pa (sle yon byed pa) to cheat, deceive, impose upon Cs.

pf. (b)slebs ((b)slebs), fut. bsleb (bsleb), resp. _byón-pa ('byon pa), péb-pa (pheb pa) (cf. _óṅ-ba ('ong ba)), 1. to arrive, with termin.; bslebs-zín (bslebs zin) I have arrived, he has arrived; in Ld. however the future *slebs yin* (*slebs yin*) is also pronounced *leb zin* (*leb zin*). — 2. to reach, to extend, to a certain place or point Pth. and col. — 3. to come in (of interest, rent, duties), hence sleb (sleb) income, revenue, public revenue, receipt of customs etc.; sleb-to (sleb tho) account of receipts.

I. sbst. v. slág-pa (slag pa). II. vb., pf. bslogs (bslogs), fut. bslog (bslog), Cs. (trs. to ldog-pa (ldog pa)) to turn, to turn round or about, to turn upside down, inside out, rkyál-pa pyi-náṅ slóg-pa (rkyal pa phyi nang slog pa) to turn out the inside of a bag; mig slóg-pa (mig slog pa) to roll one's eyes; *boṅ-bu má-lag slog _dug* (*bong bu ma lag slog 'dug*) the donkey is rolling on his back; sa slóg-pa (sa slog pa) to plough up, turn
